stem: You estimate an outcome probability from a SamplerV2 histogram and then increase the shot count from 1000 to 4000. Roughly how does the statistical (sampling) error on that estimated probability change?

options.text: It roughly halves — sampling (shot) error scales like 1/sqrt(N), so 4x the shots gives about 2x smaller error
It drops to a quarter, scaling like 1/N with the number of shots
It is essentially unchanged — statistical error depends on the hardware, not the shot count
It roughly doubles, because more shots accumulate more noise

explanation.correct: Counting outcomes is a Monte-Carlo estimate of a probability. The standard error of a frequency is sqrt(p(1-p)/N), i.e. it shrinks like 1/sqrt(N). Quadrupling the shots (1000 -> 4000) multiplies the error by 1/sqrt(4) = 1/2. This is why halving the error costs 4x the shots — the same shots<->precision trade-off that underlies the primitives.

distractors.B: Sampling error scales like 1/sqrt(N), not 1/N. You would need 16x the shots (not 4x) to quarter the error.
distractors.C: Shot noise is a property of finite sampling and clearly decreases with more shots; it is not fixed by the hardware.
distractors.D: More shots reduce, not increase, the statistical uncertainty of the estimate; the error goes down like 1/sqrt(N).
